issue_title: Since 97569e1, setUpClass is called once per test
issue_body:
Since 97569e1 `setUpClass`, `tearDownClass` `setUpModule` and `tearDownModule` get called once per unit test. This is due to running each suite's component individually, as the subprocess mode does.

According to the [unittest documentation](https://docs.python.org/2/library/unittest.html#class-and-module-fixtures) the order and number of times `setUpClass` and `tearDownClass` are called isn't strictly-speaking defined. It just so happens that by convention on unittest's own single-threaded mode that they only get called once, since the tests run in order. No guarantee is made about what happens when tests are run in a randomised order or in parallel.

Personally, I use those methods to do "expensive" set up and tear down where I know I can maintain the invariant between tests in `setUp` and `tearDown`. As such, the current behaviour makes my tests run a lot slower.

I would submit that the correct behaviour is to detect where tests are setting those methods and only divide up suites to the level where any of those methods are defined. So, for instance, if `setUpClass` was defined on a `TestCase`, then the entire `TestCase` should run in serial, but could be parallelized amongst other tests.

Here's some output to show the problem and a testcase:

from testtools import TestCase

    import sys


    class TestSetUpClass(TestCase):

        """Test set up class."""

        @classmethod
        def setUpClass(cls):
            sys.stderr.write("setUpClass\n")

        def test_one(self):
            """One"""
            sys.stderr.write("method one on two called\n")
            pass

        def test_two(self):
            """Two"""
            sys.stderr.write("method two on two called\n")
            pass

        def test_three(self):
            """Three"""
            sys.stderr.write("method three on two called\n")
            pass


    Green 1.11.0, Coverage 3.7.1, Python 2.7.9

    test.test_two.test_set_up_class
      TestSetUpClass
        OnesetUpClass
    method one on two called
    setUpClass
    method three on two called
    .   One
    .   Three
        TwosetUpClass
    method two on two called
    .   Two

    Ran 3 tests in 0.108s

    OK (passes=3)

issue_title: Support entry points for loading additional backends from third parties
issue_body:
Setuptools supplies a technique for allowing third-party packages to declare functionality in another package. These entry points could be used in Keyring to allow other packages to inject dependencies for additional backends.

This would mean that a user could easy_install (or pip install) a third party package (like my_special_keyring), and that keyring would be available (and possibly preferred based on the backend selection mechanism).

This approach may expose new security concerns, so it's worth at least some discussion about how this capability might provide additional exposure and what might be done to mitigate that exposure.

----------------------------------------
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/kang/python-keyring-lib/issue/81
- Originally reported by: Jason R. Coombs
- Originally created at: 2013-01-04T15:37:50.607

issue_title: Hashing fails after using repair.fix_face_winding
issue_body:
`repair.fix_face_winding` makes use of `np.fliplr()`, which causes the array to become not C-contiguous in memory. This means that when the `DataStore.md5()` function is called, the call to `TrackedArray.md5()` fails on the `faces` array because `hashlib` requires the array to be C-contiguous.

Proposed fix: do exactly the same thing as in `TrackedArray.crc()`: change [this line](https://github.com/mikedh/trimesh/blob/master/trimesh/util.py#L658) to

```
            if self.flags['C_CONTIGUOUS']:
                self._hashed_md5 = md5_object(self)
            else:
                # the case where we have sliced our nice
                # contiguous array into a non- contiguous block
                # for example (note slice *after* track operation):
                # t = util.tracked_array(np.random.random(10))[::-1]
                contiguous = np.ascontiguousarray(self)
                self._hashed_md5 = md5_object(contiguous)
```

issue_title: Powershell ExecutionPolicy Problem
issue_body:
OS: Windows 10
Python 3.5.1

When running  `from scapy.all import *`, a `ValueError` exception is thrown.
Here is a sample output:
```
WARNING: Windows support for scapy3k is currently in testing. Sniffing/sending/receiving packets should be working with WinPcap d
river and Powershell. Create issues at https://github.com/phaethon/scapy
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".\arper.py", line 1, in <module>
    from scapy.all import *
  File "C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\lib\python3\tools\lib\site-packages\scapy\all.py", line 16, in <module>
    from .arch import *
  File "C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\lib\python3\tools\lib\site-packages\scapy\arch\__init__.py", line 88, in <module>
    from .windows import *
  File "C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\lib\python3\tools\lib\site-packages\scapy\arch\windows\__init__.py", line 200, in <module>
    ifaces.load_from_powershell()
  File "C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\lib\python3\tools\lib\site-packages\scapy\arch\windows\__init__.py", line 151, in load_from_pow
ershell
    for i in get_windows_if_list():
  File "C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\lib\python3\tools\lib\site-packages\scapy\arch\windows\__init__.py", line 87, in get_windows_if
_list
    name, value = [ j.strip() for j in i.split(':') ]
ValueError: too many values to unpack (expected 2)
```

This occurred because in `scapy\arch\windows\__init__.py` line 78, where the powershell is started an error message saying that the profile could not be loaded due to ExecutionPolicy is shown before the output of `Get-NetAdapter`. Here is an example of that message:

```
. : File C:\Users\USERNAME\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_profile.ps1 cannot be loaded because running
scripts is disabled on this system. For more information, see about_Execution_Policies at
http://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=135170.
At line:1 char:3
+ . 'C:\Users\iluxo\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\Microsoft.PowerShell_pr ...
+   ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
    + CategoryInfo          : SecurityError: (:) [], PSSecurityException
    + FullyQualifiedErrorId : UnauthorizedAccess
```

A simple solution would be to pass an additional `-NoProfile` option when starting the powershell subprocess. This is probably a better way of doing it anyways, since if that flag is not passed, the poweshell will first load the profile and only then run the `Get-NetAdapter` command, which takes some extra time.

issue_title: Add emoji shortcuts

The hangouts web client has shortcuts for various emoji that will transform a string into a unicode emoji character. For example, `~@~` becomes `💩`, `;)` becomes `😉`, etc. The specific transformations have been documented in [this blog post](https://aprescott.com/posts/hangouts-emoji).

It would be cool to add these same shortcuts to hangups.

issue_title: ConfigurationError subclasses should provide information parts
issue_body:
Right now, Everett generates decent messages for configuration errors and throws them in the exception. That's fine for certain use cases, but there are likely use cases where instead of assembling all the information into a string, it'd be better to have the information in parts.

This issue covers reworking ConfigurationError and friends to expose the parts (namespace, key, value, error message, option help text, component help text, etc).

issue_title: Commit Confirm
issue_body: Both Junos and IOS XR support the Commit Confirm feature.  This may be a feature worth implementing it as it can save you from a lot of things.  One use case is a commit confirm with a sufficient timeout to run automated tests, at which point the automated tests (or scripts or whatever) can commit the configuration permanently.  This can save you from committing a bad change that kills connectivity to the box.

issue_title: Supports python3 ?
issue_body:
Hi,

Does pyroute2 support python3 ?
As far as I tried, it seems pyroute2 works on Python3.4. But,

* [pyroute2 pypi page](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyroute2/)
No classifiers about supported python versions.

* [Requirements section in docs](http://docs.pyroute2.org/general.html#requires)
Says only "Python >= 2.6".

I think, developers who use python3 may avoid using a library which does not announce python3 support explicitly.

If pyroute2 does support python3, **classifiers** in setup.py should have more lines like,
```
classifiers = [
    ...
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 2.6",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 2.7",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 3",
    ...
]
```
and also requirements section in docs.


Even if does not support python3, it is worth announcing only python 2 support lines like,
```
classifiers = [
    ...
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 2.6",
    "Programming Language :: Python :: 2.7",
    ...
]
```

This is just what I felt when I searched a python library to configure Linux network settings, and just a trivial suggestion.

I don't know if this is an issue with colorama or with pylint.

What steps will reproduce the problem?
1. Use colorama in a project
2. Use predefined colors
3. Run pylint on the code

Result:
E:143,18: Instance of 'AnsiCodes' has no 'RED' member (no-member)

E:187,49: Instance of 'AnsiCodes' has no 'BRIGHT' member (no-member)

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?
Code passes the check

What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?
0.3.1 on Windows via Anaconda

Please provide any additional information below.
I found a similar issue with a patch on the Chromium project:
https://codereview.chromium.org/10202010

Reported by google code user peter.azp, Nov 12, 2014

    listing_embed = discord.Embed(title=self.title, description=self.description,
                                                        color=discord.Colour(hex(random.randint(0, 16777215))),
                                                        url=self.url)

     listing_embed = discord.Embed(title=self.title, description=self.description,
                                                        color=discord.Colour(random.randint(0, 16777215)),
                                                        url=self.url)

    def __init__(self, value):

issue_title: Limit properties in a Module
issue_body:
At the moment a module doesn't check which properties are embedded in it. This is an issue if there is failed indentation and properties look like they are part of the module, not of the properties.

To fix this make sure that only `From` and `Properties` is allowed directly nested in a module.

issue_title: list.insert() doesn't trigger save
SSL/TLS certificates are not validated!
issue_body:
Hi Folks,

from my Impression current imbox does **not** validate SSL/TLS certificates.

https://github.com/martinrusev/imbox/blob/master/imbox/imap.py#L21

calls `IMAP4_SSL` default constructor which looks like:
```
imaplib.IMAP4_SSL(host='', port=IMAP4_SSL_PORT, keyfile=None, certfile=None, ssl_context=None)
```
As you can see at `ssl_context=None`. This default configuration does not validate SSL/TLS certificates!

**Using the default configuration makes software using Imbox vulnerable!**

Idea to solve this problem (untested sketch code!)
```
import ssl as pythonssllib

       if ssl:
            context = pythonssllib.create_default_context()
            if not self.port:
                self.port = 993
            self.server = self.IMAP4_SSL(self.hostname, self.port, ssl_context=context)
        else:
            if not self.port:
                self.port = 143
            self.server = self.IMAP4(self.hostname, self.port)     
```
Issuetrace: https://github.com/certtools/intelmq/issues/532

This means your software will eventually be shipped in a "long term support" release, which last from three to five years, according to the [current statistics](https://wiki.debian.org/DebianReleases#Production_Releases).

If you are interested in maintaining the package yourself upstream, that is definitely possible. You can either upload the package yourself if you are a Debian member or I can act as a sponsor when a new release is published.

issue_title: Proposal:  module init() function.
issue_body:
Due to how py3status modules are loaded their `__init__()` is called before any configuration parameters have been set.  It is often useful to run code just once when the module has been configured.

To achieve this I have often written code like this 
```
def __init__(self):
    self._initialized = False

def _init(self):
    ... run initialization code here ...
    self._initialized = True

def module_method(self):
    if not self._initialized:
        self._init()
    ... run module code here ...
```

Other modules just run code every time in their main method which only needed to be run once.

I think it would be nice if we allowed an `init()` function to be defined in a module that would be run when the config parameters had been initialised.  Similar to how `kill()` is called in a module when it is destroyed.

So a module would look like this
```
def init(self):
    ... run initialization code here ...

def module_method(self):
    ... run module code here ...
```

Adding this functionality would be trivial and would only effect existing modules if they used `init` as the name of one of their methods.

What are peoples views on this suggestion?

issue_title: Out of spec epub output (minor)
issue_body:
I ran across a site-specific ePub generator someone wrote in Javascript, of all things, which the author was quite proud of. The author claimed that what set it apart from FFF was, among other things, spec compliant output.

So I ran [epubcheck](/idpf/epubcheck) against my FFF generated epubs, and found that it was indeed out of spec, in several ways. 

First, and probably easiest to fix, is that when including images, FFF does not necessarily (never in my testing) include an alt attribute in the img element. I presume that it would pass through any alt attribute that was present in the original html, but, [according to the spec](http://www.idpf.org/epub/20/spec/OPS_2.0.1_draft.htm#Section2.3.4) the attribute is required. I did some testing, and epubcheck is satisfied with `alt=""`

Second, FFF is messing up file extensions and mime types for images, or at least for PNG images. It saves them as .jpg and (in the OPF file) image/jpeg. This is with image processing turned off, and regardless of what the default image file type is set to (which I believe wouldn't matter, as that option is for when the processing is turned on, right?)

Thirdly, FFF is passing through a few disallowed element types, including but not necessarily limited to `<center>` and `<u>`. I know that fimfiction, at least, still uses these tags, despite them being deprecated since html 4.01. My only fanfiction.net sourced story validates perfectly, so either fanfiction.net uses different elements to achieve the same styling (one would hope), or that story contains no examples of said styling.

issue_body:
On Linux with wxPython 3.x / 4.x window handle is not yet available even though Show() was called and it causes in the wxpython.py example to open two windows, one blank window and another window with CEF browser. This effect is because window handle passed to CEF is 0, thus CEF creates a top-level window on its own.

The solution is to delay embedding browser by a bit and window handle becomes available then. Time of 1ms is not enough, 10ms seems fine, but to be sure I've used 20ms. Example works fine with such code:

```
        # On Linux must show before embedding browser, so that handle
        # is available (Issue #347).
        if LINUX:
            self.Show()
            # In wxPython 3.0 and wxPython 4.0 handle is still
            # not yet available, must delay embedding browser
            # (Issue #349).
            if wx.version().startswith("3.") or wx.version().startswith("4."):
                wx.CallLater(20, self.embed_browser)
            else:
                # This works fine in wxPython 2.8
                self.embed_browser()
        else:
            self.embed_browser()
            self.Show()
```

Issue originally reported on the Forum:
https://groups.google.com/d/topic/cefpython/ZBXEdOR3svw/discussion

issue_title: Intercepting keystrokes without forwarding them
issue_body:
Hello there

I'm trying to build a basic "remapper" that takes keyboard input and relays it differently based on modes/chords or other variables.

While I mostly have a system in place which can read keystrokes and interpret them (works well so far) I haven't been able to disable the keystrokes for other purposes. i.e: If I get the keystroke for A, I can't prevent the OS from forwarding that A keystroke to the window in focus even though my program may want to send the keystroke for B or Tab or anything else.

I wonder if there's a way to make this work at least in macOS. I know that the Plover project is written in Python, and it might have the answers. I'm not so good with this stuff unfortunately so I can't really tell, but [I think here's how Plover does it on macOS](https://github.com/openstenoproject/plover/blob/master/plover/oslayer/osxkeyboardcontrol.py#L177-L233) as well as [Windows](https://github.com/openstenoproject/plover/blob/master/plover/oslayer/winkeyboardcontrol.py#L258-L280).

Would appreciate any hints.

Thanks
Adi Ron.

issue_title: A programmatic way to get to any item given its path
issue_body:
Hard-coded paths are easy:
* `config.uploads.enabled`
* `config.uploads.db.user`
* `config['uploads']['db']['user']`

But how about `path = ('uploads', 'db', 'user')` ?

Solution:

```
config[*path]
```

----

Discussion:

Re-introduction of `Config.get()` is not possible -- it would only cause confusion whether it returns an item or its value. It would have to return its value to stay consistent with `Item.get()` but that would mean you still need to provide a way to access items of a given path.

issue_title: Prevent from passing empty string as sheetname.
issue_body:
## Short description
Passing empty string as sheetname cause problems with another methods (like autofilter for me). Unfortunately I'm not Excel user. Under LibreOffice empty string is changed to "Sheet".

## Solution
As long as passing empty string does not make any sense I'm proposing to add assertion. It should not be treated as None because passing empty string (imo) is bug in application. 

## Example
~~~python
import xlsxwriter
w = xlsxwriter.Workbook('test.xlsx')
sheet = w.add_worksheet('')
sheet.write_row(0, 0, ['a', 'b'])
sheet.autofilter(0, 0, 0, 1)
w.close()
~~~

issue_title: Watch with no etcd up - Infinite loop
issue_body:
Hi, 

When we try, to add a watch but etcd is not running, there is no exception raise or status failed return.

In the next example, I try to connect the with the wrong port. No issue for client creation, but when I create my watch, etcd3 never return.

```
>>> import etcd3
>>> c=etcd3.client(host='127.0.0.1', port='2378', timeout=3)
>>> c.watch_once('1111')
```

In the watch.py file (add_callback method), the watch queue is not receiving the watcher timeout.

I think at this line:
`return self._watch_id_queue.get()`
should send the timeout value:
`return self._watch_id_queue.get(timeout=self.timeout)`

In this case, the add_callback can catch the Empty exception sent by queue.Queue.

Best regards,

issue_title: Raise exception when port given to server is already occupied
issue_body:
I am trying to implement an iperf3 server that is capable of doing multiple simultaneous tests. I am planning on doing the handling of multiple tests using Twisted. Unfortunately I've run into an issue with finding a port to give the iperf3.Server: if I give it a port that is already being used by another process, no error information is returned until the call to Server.run() returns. Until this point there is no way of telling

It seems to me that the pythonic way to do this would be to define an exception ("PortTakenError" or something similar) that is raised _before_ the call to Server.run() returns. That way, people can encapsulate the Server.run() call in a try-except statement and handle a "PortTakenError" gracefully.

Is this doable? I can probably figure out how to implement this; if I submit a pull request with these changes would you accept it?

issue_title: Django 1.9 compatibility issues
issue_body:
So far I found the following issues:

1. The imports in `post_office/__init__.py` are causing: `django.core.exceptions.AppRegistryNotReady: Apps aren't loaded yet.` in validation, before the tests are running.

2. The structure of `django.core.mail.backends.base.BaseBackend` changed somehow and the import is broken, easy fix.

3. `django.utils.log.dictConfig` was removed. Can maybe replaced with `logging.config.dictConfig` (Python 2.6 support must be dropped), or doing something like: https://github.com/nvie/rq/commit/77fb32791d6fa219b4b08469e252cedbd08f964b. Since Django 1.9 isn't supposed to work with Python 2.6, the best would be to fallback from one to another.

4. Django template error messages were changed causing failed assertions in test_invalid_syntax() - easy fix.

I can fix 2 - 4, but no idea what to do with 1.

issue_title: require_module template tag doesn't reference hashed file
issue_body:
Using django-require v1.0.11.

Testing with the official storage:

    STATICFILES_STORAGE = 'require.storage.OptimizedManifestStaticFilesStorage'

I have a single standalone module defined:

        'main': {
            'out': 'main-built.js',
            'build_profile': 'app.build.js',
        }
    }

I run collectstatic to run the r.js optimizer on my bundle and I get a `js/main-built.19882680b8d2.js` in my `STATIC_ROOT` directory. I can also see the corresponding entry in the `staticfiles.json`:

    "js\\main-built.js": "js/main-built.19882680b8d2.js"

So far, so good!

Now I reference this in my template:

    {% require_module "main" %}

I set `REQUIRE_DEBUG = False` and load my page in the browser.

I expected the following line to be generated:

    <script src="http://localhost:8000/static/js/main-built.19882680b8d2.js"></script>

Instead, I got this:

    <script src="http://localhost:8000/static/js/main-built.js"></script>

Did I miss something? What do I have to do for `require_module` to return hashed URLs?

issue_title: How to whitelist bots/IP addresses?
issue_body:
I put ratelimits in place last week, and now I'm getting errors from Google that it can't crawl as fast as it wants to, and is getting 429 errors as a result.

The easiest fix would be to have a whitelist of IP addresses that are allowed to crawl. That'd work for partner organizations with static IPs, but according to [Google's support page][google], the Googlebot's IP address changes from time to time, so you are expected to verify it by using the `host` command.

So: is there a way to whitelist Google's IP addresses or to run a custom function for whitelisting? 

I could see an API like this for the simple case:

    @ratelimit(key='ip', rate='100/h', block=True, whitelist=[some-list-of-ip-addresses])

And like this for the hard case of having to verify the crawler:

    @ratelimit(key='ip', rate='100/h', block=True, whitelist=is_whitelisted)

The callable would then look something like this:

    def is_whitelisted(request):
        """Check if the IP is in our list of trusted static IPs or if it's Google."""
        ip = request.get('IP_ADDRESS'))
        if ip in settings['WHITELIST']:
            return True
        # Not in our static whitelist. Is it Google?
        is_google = verify_google_by_hostname(ip)
        return is_google

Is there an easy way to handle this? If not would pull requests be welcome? I might be able to find time to do this, though I know I'll struggle to figure out how to pass the request object to the whitelist callable if we go with this API (I'm no good at making wrappers).

Thoughts?

[google]: https://support.google.com/webmasters/answer/80553?hl=en

    in1 = ffmpeg.input('1.mkv')
    in2 = ffmpeg.input('2.mkv')
    ffmpeg.concat(in1, in2).output('out.mp4').run()

results in the correct video but with no audio information (ffmpeg reports audio=0kB even).

Running this:

    ffmpeg.input('1.mkv').output('out.mp4').run()

successfully converts 1.mkv to an mp4 file while preserving the audio

issue_title: Create a framework for extensions with plugins
issue_body:
Starting a thread to continue what started in #56. The basic idea is to create different signals and emit them at different points of the code. Plugins subscribe to different signals as a means to inject various executions to achieve their target.

[Blinker](https://pythonhosted.org/blinker/) is a popular library to implement signals in python.

Ping @ninadmhatre

Basic design principle:

We create a `signals.py` that defines different entry points. For example:

```
before_get_post_by_id = signal("before_get_post_by_id")
after_get_post_by_id = signal("after_get_post_by_id")
```

Now in the `get_post_by_id(...)` function call in `sqlastorage.py` we send signals, `before_get_post_by_id` at the beginning of the function and `after_get_post_by_id` for at the end of the function.

Now if you wanted to get extra data from the database in your plugin, after the post data was fetched by id, then you can inject your code by subscribing to `after_get_post_by_id` call. 

This is the gist of the idea. We still need to discuss what signals to send (which can change over time). There are some other details. But this is a good start.

[Flask-Security](https://github.com/mattupstate/flask-security/blob/develop/flask_security/signals.py) implements all the signals here. You can follow the package to get a sense of how they use signals at different points in the code.

[Pelican plugin](http://docs.getpelican.com/en/3.6.3/plugins.html#how-to-create-plugins) documentaion is a good resource as well.

issue_title: Make test suite fail if benchmark is unsatisfactory
issue_body:
Hello,
I read the documentation and hope I did not miss something completely obvious.

My use case: I would like to use `pytest-benchmark` for continuous integration. A part of my test suite is actually performance tests, i.e. making sure that my `toto` function does not take longer than, let's say, 20ms.

I would like the test suite to *fail* is some modifications in the code make `toto()` exceeds the 20ms. I am aware of the `--benchmark-compare-fail=EXPR`, but I think what I am looking for is more specific.

I have no idea what would be the best way to describe this, perhaps:
```python
@pytest.mark.benchmark(fail_at=0.02)
def test_toto(benchmark):
    benchmark(toto)
```
Or maybe:
```python
def test_toto(benchmark):
    results = benchmark(toto, fail_at=0.02)
```
Or provide a way for the user to access the results of the benchmark? Before using `pytest-benchmark`, I would do something like this:
```python
def test_toto(benchmark):
    results = benchmark(toto)
    if results.average_time > 0.02:
        pytest.fail('Exceeding 20ms!')
```
Would this make sense in `pytest-benchmark`?

issue_title: Ignore open_url=True when live-reloading the server
issue_body:
I've a `live.py` that looks like this:

```python
from livereload import Server

server = Server()
# no recursive globbing on Python 2 :(
for pattern in '*.rst', '*/*.rst':
    server.watch(pattern, 'make html')
server.serve(root='_build/html', open_url=True)
```

Every time I make a change to it, I see

>  [I 150408 13:13:54 autoreload:195] live.py modified; restarting server

on the console, and then I get a new browser tab.

issue_title: Pip install not up to date
issue_body:
A friend and I have discovered an alarming behavior, where running the exact same code, but having installed neat from pip vs from github, gives differing behaviors. I tested this with my friend @HiggstonRainbird and we decided it merited opening an issue. To replicate, you can clone the following repo: [https://github.com/thecog19/neat-bug-demo](https://github.com/thecog19/neat-bug-demo )

On a computer running linux, having installed neat .91 with pip install, run

``` python evolve-feedforward_fizzbuzz.py ```

The network will never reach an answer, choosing to, instead, de-evolve every few hundred iterations, as so: [http://i.imgur.com/mBMoQKr.png](http://i.imgur.com/mBMoQKr.png)

When instead neat is installed via the github repo, the network will solve the problem within 2000 generations, usually closer to 1000. 

This is obviously undesirable behavior, and it caused us many headaches, so we thought we'd share.

Installing zinnia to a production system, fails on `zinnia.0003_publication_date` migration with some timeout. 

It looks that `fill_publication_date()` function in "/zinnia/migrations/0003_publication_date.py" timeouts when trying to access entries with: `for entry in entry_klass.objects.all()`, but zinnia database is empty.

Exception follows:

Running migrations:

  Applying zinnia.0003_publication_date...Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./manage.py", line 28, in <module>
    execute_from_command_line(sys.argv)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 364, in execute_from_command_line
    utility.execute()

    self.fetch_command(subcommand).run_from_argv(self.argv)
 line 283, in run_from_argv
    self.execute(*args, **cmd_options)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py", line 330, in execute
    output = self.handle(*args, **options)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/core/management/commands/migrate.py", line 204, in handle
    fake_initial=fake_initial,
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/migrations/executor.py", line 115, in migrate
    state = self._migrate_all_forwards(state, plan, full_plan, fake=fake, fake_initial=fake_initial)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/migrations/executor.py", line 145, in _migrate_all_forwards
    state = self.apply_migration(state, migration, fake=fake, fake_initial=fake_initial)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/migrations/executor.py", line 244, in apply_migration
    state = migration.apply(state, schema_editor)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/migrations/migration.py", line 129, in apply
    operation.database_forwards(self.app_label, schema_editor, old_state, project_state)
  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/db/migrations/operations/special.py", line 193, in database_forwards

  File "/home/user/project_root/env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/zinnia/migrations/0003_publication_date.py", line 7, in fill_publication_date
    for entry in entry_klass.objects.all():

Migrations, especially for a fresh zinnia installation without entries in database, should not raise time out exceptions, breaking also a deployment.

  2. python manage.py migrate (or python manage.py migrate zinnia, same output).

issue_title: 2.0 shuts down application queue futures before delivering `websocket.disconnect` message
issue_body:
I am testing 2.0 and found my websocket cleanup code was not being called.

In the Twisted `onClose` handling at https://github.com/django/daphne/blob/26fa870540f175eb4046a836abb465ebf1a14612/daphne/ws_protocol.py#L129 `discard_protocol` is called and the application queue future is being shut down. This prevents the `websocket.disconnect` message from being processed.

My guess is: the application queue should shut down after it is completely processed.

It can be reproduced by creating a `WebsocketConsumer` and checking whether `disconnect` is called.

Will look into a PR later in the week if I can re-wrap my head in asyncio; it has been a while :)

issue_title: New PyPI Release?
issue_body:
Hi, I'm struggling to get a project off the ground. The project depends on another project, which ultimately depends on django-appdata. Was going fine, until we ran Python 3.x tests. Turns out, appdata version 0.1.3 is not really Py3 compatible. I know these issues have been fixed, because my project works if I force install django-appdata from the tip of the repo. Unfortunately, since its not yet in PyPI, I don't know how to get my project to test cleanly on Travis (or other) without cheating. Then, eventually, our users will run into the same issue anyway.

Is there anyway we could get a new release to PyPI that at least tests cleanly on Py3? It'd be a huge help to us.

Many thanks!

(just tagging some others from my team here: @evildmp, @yakky)

issue_body:
The `inproc://` transport seems to not work on Windows with some patterns, for instance in [this threaded version](https://gist.github.com/chancyk/41f1028cb444a9ffed404c5fc9fe33c3) of the lbbroker example found in the ZeroMQ guide [here](http://zguide.zeromq.org/py:lbbroker). issue_body:
Without some way of releasing SOPS in easily installable packages it starts to feel more like an experiment rather then a fully fledged application. Requiring that users install and setup Go seems unnecessary burden especially since go can compile to native binaries.

- Have packages
-- OSX: macports or homebrew packages, maybe even .pkg files at some point
-- RHEL/CENTOS: have RPM packages
-- Debian/Ubuntu: have apt packages
-- Windows: msi installs

In the meantime maybe having automated github releases of binaries with installation instructions would be a good stopgap solution. These could be built on travis and pushed directly to github.com/mozilla/sops automatically

issue_title: option to collapse fully-sanitized lines when cleaning
issue_body:
(this is an idea brought up for discussion)

Bleach sanitizes content in-place, which is the ideal behavior for most situations, allowing a "line for line" comparison of input and output.

In certain situations, it may be desirable to omit those lines which become "empty" because of sanitization

    >>> line = "one
two
<!-- foo -->
three

four"
    >>> bleach.clean(line)
    u'one
two

three

four'

In the case above, a user might want the output to not include the `

` between "two" and "three" as it was caused by a stripped node occurring both after & before a newline.

    u'one
two
three

four'

issue_title: Release for every feature/bug fix
issue_body:
I propose we make releases more frequently. One release for every new feature or bug fix. That way, we don't have have a meeting and say "Ok, what's changed since the last release?" and type that up in `HISTORY.txt` along with a change to `mozilla_django_oidc/__init__.py`'s `__version__` string. 

It is possible to encryt your PyPI password and put that in `.travis.yml` and let travis try to make a PyPI release on every successful build on origin/master. If the version number hasn't change, PyPI will simply respond with a gentle error like "Sorry, that version already exists". 
That means if all you do is commit a change to the documentation or a style fix or a change only to the unit tests, no new PyPI release is made. 

This would require a change of how you make PRs. If your PR indeed contains a feature change or a bug fix, you're *also* expected to edit `HISTORY.txt` and `__init__.py` along with your change. 
If two people make a PR based on version 1.2.3 and they both make PRs that change `HISTORY.txt` and `__init__.py` then it's likely to cause a conflict and the author has to rebase up. 

Another option is to still set up travis to push to PyPI on every origin/master build, but NOT require PRs to contain a change to `HISTORY.txt` and `__init__.py`. Then once your feature/bug fix lands on master you make *another* PR that just edits `HISTORY.txt` and `__init__.py`. 

Thoughts? Ideas? Objections? Endorsements?

issue_title: Behavior of file metadata
issue_body:
Even though the metadata class subclasses `dict`, the typical `dict` methods like `update` do not work with it (properly), e.g.
```
my_files = api.files.query(project=project_id, names=[fname])
my_files[0].metadata.update(new_metadata)
my_files[0].save()
print(my_files[0].metadata)
```
Does not actually save the new metadata to the platform, although the last step seems to print the updated metadata that the user wishes had gone to the platform.

My best guess is that it has to do with [the `__setitem__` method](https://github.com/sbg/sevenbridges-python/blob/master/sevenbridges/models/compound/metadata.py#L16-L18) in which the `__setitem__` method of the superclass is called, but is that method call coming from `dict` or `Resource`?

issue_title: What is the point of target_table in sqla.SQLAlchemyTarget?
issue_body:
Hi,

I was exploring contrib.sqla for using `SQLAlchemyTarget` without `CopyToTable`. 
(Not sure how recommended this is, but that is not the point here)

I was wondering about the point of the `target_table` constructor argument and instance property.
It is used in `touch()` for insert/update in the marker table
but is not read/checked somewhere (e.g. it could be added as where clause in `exists()`)

For my use case, the target_table is not interesting (I use "dummy"), so I'd like to have it dropped,
but I guess for users of `CopyToTable` covering multiple target tables, this might be a source of bugs (wrong results from `exists()`).

issue_title: Add a job queue
issue_body:
Now that Celery isn't used by most servers (they use multiprocess) we do not have any queue. This is increasing issue with computational resources. We should start thinking about a 'smart' job queue:

- We have e huge server booked and reserved for just exporting data (which is using 1 core and few MB of RAM)
- We have more than one user on a single machine: with celery the task are in a queue (which may be sub-optimal), with multiprocess everything tries to run with JOBS*CPU processes (2 jobs on a 32 cores machine are generating 64 processing trying to use 100% of CPU) causing a general slowdown ad high load average
- It's not easy to create an external queue right now, because external queue manager is not aware of in which phase the calculation is and then which resources is using/going to use.

issue_title: Cloud Directory should accept None as NextToken
issue_body:
Let's say your listing and index or something, and want to iterate the pages:

```python
next = None
while True:
    resp = client.list_index(
        DirectoryArn='...',
        RangesOnIndexedValues=[...]
        IndexReference={'Selector': '..'},
        NextToken=next
    )
    next = resp.get('NextToken')
    if not next:
        break
```

The above doesn't work as boto doesn't accept `None` as NextToken value (it nags about it not being string, iirc). So I would have to:

```python
next = None
while True:
    if next:
        resp = client.list_index(
            DirectoryArn='...',
            RangesOnIndexedValues=[...]
            IndexReference={'Selector': '..'},
            NextToken=next
        )
    else:
        resp = client.list_index(
            DirectoryArn='...',
            RangesOnIndexedValues=[...]
            IndexReference={'Selector': '..'}
            # omit it here
        )

    next = resp.get('NextToken')
    if not next:
        break
```

Which is pretty annoying. Workaround is to:

```python
next = None
while True:
    params = dict(
        DirectoryArn='...',
        RangesOnIndexedValues=[...]
        IndexReference={'Selector': '..'}
    )
    if next_token:
        params['NextToken'] = next
    resp = client.list_index(**params)

    next = resp.get('NextToken')
    if not next:
        break
```

Which is pretty ugly, plus you lose auto-completion (which actually doesn't exist due to https://github.com/boto/boto3/issues/1055).

$ python3.4
[GCC 4.2.1 Compatible Apple LLVM 6.0 (clang-600.0.54)] on darwin

  File "/opt/local/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/site-packages/pygit2/_utils.py", line 107, in get_ffi
    include_dirs=[libgit2_include], library_dirs=[libgit2_lib])

issue_title: Unexpected behaviour of .unflatten method. BUG?
issue_body:
Some code chunk:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

from __future__ import print_function

import copy
import pprint

from colander import MappingSchema
from colander import SchemaNode
from colander import String
from colander import drop


class StrictMappingSchema(MappingSchema):
    @staticmethod
    def schema_type():
        return MappingSchema.schema_type(unknown='raise')


class StrictSchema(StrictMappingSchema):
    foo = SchemaNode(String(), type='str', missing=drop)
    bar = SchemaNode(String(), type='str')


class NestedSchema(MappingSchema):
    egg = StrictSchema()
    ham = StrictSchema()


data = {'egg.bar': 'GET', 'ham': {'bar': 'POST'}}

schema = NestedSchema()
print('original data:')
pprint.pprint(data)

print('\nunflattened data:')
unflattened = copy.deepcopy(data)
pprint.pprint(schema.unflatten(unflattened))
```

It's output:
```
original data:
{'egg.bar': 'GET', 'ham': {'bar': 'POST'}}

unflattened data:
{'egg': {'bar': 'GET'}, 'ham': {}}
```

As you can see - we have lost content of original 'ham' key. Is it a bug or just .unflatten feature?

issue_title: Allow construction-time hooks to pass external tracing information to each request
issue_body:
Scenario: an http server that is instrumented for Zipkin tracing and has a notion of the "current span". If such service makes a downstream call over TChannel, we don't want TChannel to start a new trace/span, but to start the new span as a child of the existing span established by the http server.

I am not sure if today it's possible to do this bridging by passing `parent_tracing` to each `call()`, but even if it's possible, it requires manually changing the application at every call site. Instead, it would be much cleaner if the app owner could set a certain "tracing hook" when creating the TChannel, so that this hook would get executed for every request and would retrieve the current parent span from http request context (by whatever means), and return the `parent_tracing` to the current request (or alternatively set it as the current `parent_tracing` in TChannel's RequestContext thread-local.

issue_title: New keyword "Get Browser Capabilities".
issue_body:
This keyword was initially name "Get Browser Info" developed in PR #482 .
This keyword will be the base for the keyword "Get Browser Version" as requested on #569 .

The keyword by design returns a dictionary of Capabilities. This dictionary is DotDict, meaning that capabilities can be addressed either by index or by attributes. For example:
```
| ${capabilities}= | Get Browser Capabilities                            |                           |
| Log              | ${capabilities.browserName} ${capabilities.version} | # Using attributes        |
| Log              | Capabilities = ${capabilities}                      |                           |
| Log              | ${capabilities['platformName']}                     | # Using dictionary        |
| Log              | ${capabilities.get('may_not_exist', 'default')}     | # Gets 'default'          |
| Log              | ${capabilities['may_not_exist']}                    | # Produces KeyError       |
| Log              | ${capabilities.may_not_exist}                       | # Produces AttributeError |

```

issue_title: with dependency link to an hg repo, install fails to switch to correct revision (windows)
issue_body:
Originally reported by: **ccanepa (Bitbucket: [ccanepa](http://bitbucket.org/ccanepa), GitHub: [ccanepa](http://github.com/ccanepa))**

---

While testing the build for a new release of library cocos, http://code.google.com/p/los-cocos/ , I found a problem with a dependency, the pyglet library , http://code.google.com/p/pyglet/ .

With dependency link to hg repo, install fails to switch to correct revision.

It clones the repo, the dependency gets installed, but it is at the tip revision, not the one specified.

I think the relevant part of 'setup.py install' output is

```
#!

        Doing hg clone from https://code.google.com/p/pyglet to c:\docume~1\jugar\config
        ~1\temp\easy_install-icuiyz\pyglet@82693a20ece0
        Updating to 82693a20ece0
        No se esperaba >& en este momento.
        Processing pyglet@82693a20ece0  

```

'Updating to 82693a20ece0' seems to be emitted by setuptools before calling hg to do an hg update, ( yes, in setuptools.package_index.PackageIndex._download_hg , line 855 )

The line 'No se esperaba >& en este momento.' is comming from the OS, the meaning being 'unexpected >&'

In line 856 theres

```
#!python

 os.system("(cd %s && hg up -C -r %s >&-)" % (
```

Testing in a console standing in a pyglet's checkout I see

```
#!shell

hg up -C -r 82693a20ece0
```

works fine
whereas

```
#!shell

    D:\hg_externals\pyglet_dev>hg up -C -r 82693a20ece0 >&-
    No se esperaba >& en este momento.

```

So, the redirection in that os.system call is not working in Windows.

What is that ? >dev/null ?  That would be >nul in classic Window, and $null in Windows's Powershell.

Would it be acceptable to replace the redirect with a '-q' (quiet) ?
That is, the line 

```
#!python

           os.system("(cd %s && hg up -C -r %s >&-)" % (

```

changes to

```
#!python

            os.system("(cd %s && hg up -C -r %s -q)" % (

```

Details:
Running on windows xp
cocos package distribution built (from an export of revision r1357) both with py 2.6 and 3.3, both with setuptools 3.3; looks good.

The failure was observed when installed into a python 2.7 + setuptools 3.3

hg version is 2.9.1

---
- Bitbucket: https://bitbucket.org/pypa/setuptools/issue/170

issue_title: The `--install-option` option in a requirements.txt file leaks to all later packages being installed
issue_body:
setuptools 28.3.0 contains a regression wherein if you ran `pip install -r requirements.txt` and the `requirements.txt` file made use of the `--install-option` feature like the following:

```
talon==1.3.2 --install-option="--no-ml"
cchardet==1.0.0
cssselect==0.9.2
lxml==3.6.1
regex==2016.7.21
```

setuptools would end up passing the `--no-ml` option to not just `talon` (in this case) but also all packages listed later in the requirements.txt file, giving errors like this:

```
  Running setup.py install for cchardet ... error
    Complete output from command /srv/zulip-venv-cache/8e84e610d0834b666d337a42b7cd5085b13331af/zulip-venv/bin/python -u -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-9f_SEG/cchardet/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-JdIYOA-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /srv/zulip-venv-cache/8e84e610d0834b666d337a42b7cd5085b13331af/zulip-venv/include/site/python2.7/cchardet --no-ml:
    usage: -c [global_opts] cmd1 [cmd1_opts] [cmd2 [cmd2_opts] ...]
       or: -c --help [cmd1 cmd2 ...]
       or: -c --help-commands
       or: -c cmd --help
    
    error: option --no-ml not recognized
    
    ----------------------------------------
Command "/srv/zulip-venv-cache/8e84e610d0834b666d337a42b7cd5085b13331af/zulip-venv/bin/python -u -c "import setuptools, tokenize;__file__='/tmp/pip-build-9f_SEG/cchardet/setup.py';exec(compile(getattr(tokenize, 'open', open)(__file__).read().replace('\r\n', '\n'), __file__, 'exec'))" install --record /tmp/pip-JdIYOA-record/install-record.txt --single-version-externally-managed --compile --install-headers /srv/zulip-venv-cache/8e84e610d0834b666d337a42b7cd5085b13331af/zulip-venv/include/site/python2.7/cchardet --no-ml" failed with error code 1 in /tmp/pip-build-9f_SEG/cchardet/
```

See https://github.com/zulip/zulip/issues/1959 for details.

I was able to fix the issue by downgrading to 27.3.1; I haven't bisected yet to see the precise version that introduced the issue, but I certainly can if that's helpful.

issue_body:
I'm building tools and libraries that allow people to use Minio/S3 storage with deep learning frameworks. This means that users are going to switch freely between writing Python code, Go code, and command line tools. This involves a lot of end user programming and scripting.

It would be nice if these different tools could share a common authentication infrastructure. In particular, it would be convenient if minio-py had an option of taking its keys and path shortcuts from ~/.mc/config.json

Of course, I can write a wrapper library, but having the option of having consistent paths and key management between the different Minio libraries and command line tools would seem to be a nice feature to have.

issue_title: cygwin下编译失败
Argument parsing for build_ext command is generally broken
issue_body:
Over in astropy/astropy#4277 I discovered that when running the setup.py build_ext command with some command-line options, such as

```
$ ./setup.py build_ext --inplace --use-system-wcslib
```

the code that tests whether or not the `--use-system-wcslib` flag was enabled fails.  The command still runs, and uses the `--inplace` flag correctly.  But any code that tries to read the command line flags *prior* to the `setup()` function running (for example [`astropy.wcs.setup_package`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/master/astropy/wcs/setup_package.py)) will fail.

This problem is specific to the `build_ext` command, but exposes a more general problem with `./setup.py` command-line parsing.  In fact the reason this broke was because of some horrible hacks I had to perform in #185 in order to deal with the subtleties of distutils command-line handling.

I'm going to try to find a new workaround, but I thought I would use this as an opportunity to explain how distutils command-line handling works, and why it's difficult to deal with.

In distutils, each of the individual commands--sub-commands of the `setup.py` script such as `setup.py install`, `setup.py build`, etc. are implemented as *classes*.  These classes have a few class-level attributes on them (namely one called `user_options`) that define what command-line arguments can be accepted by that command.  When it parses the arguments passed to a sub-command, it requires that each of those arguments is explicitly defined on the command class.  Otherwise command-line parsing fails completely.

By default distutils (and its extension setuptools) comes with several commands pre-defined.  However, it is possible to add *new* commands, or replace existing commands with a new command of the same name, by registering new command classes with distutils.  When replacing an existing command it is also common-place to subclass the original implementation of that command in order to add new functionality.  astropy-helpers for example subclasses several of the built-in commands in order to add new functionality (or in some cases work around bugs): https://github.com/astropy/astropy-helpers/tree/master/astropy_helpers/commands

(By far one of the more complicated subclassed commands in astropy-helpers is `build_ext`, which adds significant functionality for making it easier to deal with Cython extensions--we'll get back to that.)

New/replacement commands are registered with distutils by passing an argument called `cmdclass` to the `setup()` function.  `cmdclass` should be a dict mapping the name of the command (e.g. `'install'`) to the class that implements that command (which can have any name).  Because this happens "late" in the `setup.py`--calling `setup()` being the last thing that happens in any `setup.py`--it is impossible to properly parse the `./setup.py` command-line arguments any time before `setup()` is called unless we know ahead of time what command classes *will* be registered with distutils.

This is why astropy-helpers has a function called [`register_commands`](https://github.com/astropy/astropy-helpers/blob/v1.1b1/astropy_helpers/setup_helpers.py#L269) that is called early in the setup.py.  This returns a dictionary of all commands that astropy-helpers adds/extends.  This is created early on so that astropy-helpers can then use distutils' command-line parsing in order for individual sub-packages to make decisions based on what command-line arguments the user provided (for example, the `astropy.wcs` package has to be configured differently depending on whether the `--use-system-wcslib` argument was provided).  This works okay, but is pretty kludgy.  It also makes it hard for affiliated packages to further add/extend commands.  They have to provide their own wrapper around `register_commands` in order for it to really work with astropy-helpers.

(To be continued...)

issue_title: RFC oauth2server: defining scopes in registry
issue_body: **Issue by [lnielsen-cern](https://github.com/lnielsen-cern)** _Friday Jun 06, 2014 at 09:04 GMT_ _Originally opened as https://github.com/jirikuncar/invenio/issues/301_ ---- **Question:** How should Invenio modules define OAuth 2.0 scopes so that ``invenio.modules.oauth2server`` knowns about them? **Problem:** An Invenio module may provide a REST API in ``restful.py`` see e.g. ``invenio.modules.deposit.restful``. The REST API may require authentication using OAuth 2.0. If authentication is required, it may also require specific OAuth scopes to be present in the OAuth access token. Here's a simple example of how that looks in ``restful.py`` file: ```python class SomeRESTResource(Resource): @require_api_auth() @require_oauth_scopes('someresource:write') def post(self, oauth): # ... def setup_app(app, api): api.add_resource(SomeRESTResource, '/api/myresource/') ``` Here the required scope is ``someresource:write``. Also, the ``setup_app`` is called on initialization to register the REST resource (similar to the blueprints). The OAuth scopes must be registered so that ``invenio.modules.oauth2server`` knows all possible scopes for the current running application (to e.g. present them to the end user). **Proposed solution A:** Register scopes during setup_app in ``restful.py``: ```python from invenio.modules.oauth2server.registry import scopes def setup_app(app, api): api.add_resource(SomeRESTResource, '/api/myresource/') scopes.register(Scope('someresource:write', 'Write my resource')) ``` **Proposed solution B:** Register scopes in ``scopes.py`` (or ``scopesext.py``): ```python scopes = [('someresource:write','Write my resource)] ``` **Proposed solution C:** File structure: ``` mymod/oauth2serverext/__init__.py mymod/oauth2serverext/scopes/__init__.py mymod/oauth2serverext/scopes/someresource_write.py ``` and ``someresource_write.py`` would contain: ```python scope = 'someresource:write' help_text = 'Write access to some reosurce' ``` Main difference between (B) and (C) is that (C) has one file per scope vs (B) which has one file per module containing all scopes. **Proposed solution (D):** ```python # mymodule1/restful.py class SomeRESTResource(Resource): @require_api_auth() @require_oauth_scopes(Scope("preferences:write", help=_("Write to your preferences"), Scope("email:read", help=_("Read your email address") ) def post(self, oauth): # ... @oauth.require_scopes('preferences:write') def put(self, oauth): # ... # mymodule2/restful.py class AnotherRESTResource(Resource): @require_oauth_scopes(Scope("anotherresource:write", help=_("Write to your resource"), "email:read") def post(self, oauth): # ... ``` **Note:** Note, this RFC is not about how the scope string (i.e. ``someresource:write`` in the example above) should look like.

issue_title: Interleaving images
issue_body: GalSim's build-in methods to generate surface brightness profiles, for e.g. ```galsim.Gaussian```, ```galsim.OpticalPSF``` etc., can be explicitly convolved with the native pixel response and then drawn using a smaller pixel scale to give images that are finely sampled. However, when pixel level effects, like non-linearity and IPC have to be incorporated, then the images have to be drawn at the native pixel scale, which are typically sampled below Nyquist rate. Highly sampled images including these pixel level effects can be obtained from a dither sequence with uniform offsets by interleaving them, without having to invoke external image combination softwares.

issue_title: Pupil Planes can be too big for galsim.optics
issue_body:
In galsim.optics you can put in a highres pupil image and the code will fail, whereas if you deres the image (say, take every 4 of it), the code will run.


```python
import galsim.optics
from astropy.io import fits
resample_factor = 1
pupil_plane_data = fits.getdata('DECam_pupil_512.fits')
# the pupil is oversampled relative to PSF by 8
if resample_factor > 1:
    pupil_plane_data = pupil_plane_data[::resample_factor, ::resample_factor]
pupil_plane_im = galsim.Image(pupil_plane_data)
opt = galsim.optics.OpticalPSF(aberrations=[0,0,0,0,0.5,0.1], diam=4, lam=700, pupil_plane_im=pupil_plane_im, oversampling=int(8 / resample_factor))
```

Which if you run, you get:

```python
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
RuntimeError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-8-30326ca7dfa5> in <module>()
      8     pupil_plane_data = pupil_plane_data[::resample_factor, ::resample_factor]
      9 pupil_plane_im = galsim.Image(pupil_plane_data)
---> 10 opt = galsim.optics.OpticalPSF(aberrations=[0,0,0,0,0.5,0.1], diam=4, lam=700, pupil_plane_im=pupil_plane_im, oversampling=oversampling)

ons, circular_pupil, obscuration, interpolant, oversampling, pad_factor, suppress_warning, max_size, flux, nstruts, strut_thick, strut_angle, pupil_plane_im, pupil_angle, scale_unit
, gsparams)
    331             aberrations=aberrations, circular_pupil=circular_pupil, obscuration=obscuration,

--> 333             pupil_plane_im=pupil_plane_im, pupil_angle=pupil_angle, oversampling=oversampling)

/u/ki/cpd/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/galsim/optics.pyc in psf_image(array_shape, scale, lam_over_diam, aberrations, circular_pupil, obscuration, nstruts, strut_thick, strut_
angle, flux, pupil_plane_im, pupil_angle, oversampling)
    933         if oversamp_ratio < 0.9:

issue_title: Two site / two cluster config
issue_body:
Hello,

A common use case which I currently implement often is as follows:

Site A:
 - node A1
 - node A2
 - node A3

Site D (DR site):
 - node D1
 - node D2
 - node D3

Site A would be configured as a normal Patroni cluster.

Site D would be configured as a master-less cluster with D1 attached to A(master) and D2 and D3 cascading to D1.

Ideally I would actually have D2 and D3 replicating from D(lock owner which wasn't a master) - but I don't have this today.

Is there a way to do this in Patroni, or can anyone suggest somewhere I could concentrate some effort to make it work?

The requirements are:

- Two 'sites' A/D
- A is fully protected by automatic failover within site, but not between sites (manual failover to D only)
- D has no master under normal operation, with one node connected to A and the other two nodes cascading
- Failover to D is a single command, which brings up a node as master (and removes the cascading links if needed)

Cheers,
James

issue_title: pass postgresql connect_address to callbacks
issue_body: it would be good to dynamically create consul services, to pass the connect_address of postgresql to the callback scripts, so that it will be easier to make the postgresql master available over consul dns.

issue_title: file completion not working after redirection
issue_body:
Here is the help message:

```
clipr --help
clipr

Usage:
  clipr input
  clipr output
  clipr version
  clipr --help

Options:
  --help    Print this message.

Subcommands:
  input:        Put content from stdin into clipboard.
  output:       Put content from clipboard into stdin.
  version:      Print version.
```

If I type `clipr output > /home/k` then tab, there is no suggestion at all.

issue_title: 3.0.2 printing tons of debug on start
issue_body:
i'm using Django 1.7.9 together with Opbeat and [django-configurations](https://github.com/jezdez/django-configurations). Whenever configurations.management.execute_from_command_line (from django-configurations) is called with the sys.argv in manage.py I get the output below. This happens with Opbeat 3.0.2 but not with Opbeat 2.0.1 so I think this might be a problem with Opbeat? I don't see anything wrong in the application, but this is quite annoying as you might imagine.

I have a script that compiles some Django classes to [RAML](http://raml.org) which uses configurations.importer.install and django.apps.apps.populate that does not show this debug log.

issue_title: htop shortcuts
issue_body: Guake interferes with htop's shortcuts. Particularly F4.

issue_title: [crash] 'CompactNode' object has no attribute 'assiette'
issue_body:
With the latest `v0.5dev` on openfisca-web-api (659dac8e75bb409afede114f59c5fb3a8d86a5e6), openfisca-france (78ec32edbd0e7897960de714c95da345ffe765e5) and openfisca-core (555e99685f16b85d6f89c21ba1ff7689ba664524), I get a crash when I request the calculation of `allegement_fillon` (was `alleg_fillon` < 0.5).

The seemingly important parts of the trace are:

```log
14:57:59,557 ERROR [openfisca_core.formulas] An error occurred while calling formula individus@reintegration_titre_restaurant_employeur<2015-02> in openfisca_france.model.cotisations_sociales.remuneration_prive.reintegration_titre_restaurant_employeur
14:57:59,558 ERROR [openfisca_core.formulas] An error occurred while calling formula individus@salbrut<2015-02> in openfisca_france.model.cotisations_sociales.remuneration_prive.salbrut
14:57:59,558 ERROR [openfisca_core.formulas] An error occurred while calling formula individus@ratio_smic_salaire<2015> in openfisca_france.model.cotisations_sociales.allegements.function_2012
14:57:59,558 ERROR [openfisca_core.formulas] An error occurred while calling formula individus@allegement_fillon_annuel<2015> in openfisca_france.model.cotisations_sociales.allegements.allegement_fillon_annuel
14:57:59,558 ERROR [openfisca_core.formulas] An error occurred while calling formula individus@allegement_fillon<2015-01> in openfisca_france.model.cotisations_sociales.allegements.allegement_fillon

[…]

File "/Users/sgmap/Dev/openfisca-france/openfisca_france/model/cotisations_sociales/remuneration_prive.py", line 145, in function
simulation.legislation_at(period.start).cotsoc.assiette.cantines_titres_restaurants.taux_minimum_exoneration
AttributeError: 'CompactNode' object has no attribute 'assiette'
```

**This kills the server and all possible proxies, as the output is an HTML Internal Server Error and not a JSON output**, even though the requested content-type is JSON.

The full trace is available [here](https://gist.github.com/MattiSG/91cc73e0f8cf76ddf094) and not shown here for brevity (99 lines).

This regression happened in the past 10 days.

issue_title: Keep web API version in Piwik stats
issue_body: In order to check web API versions usage, keep the api version (as v18) in the requested url sent to Piwik. To do so, see `request.url` in core web api module (`app.py`).

issue_title: AL Primo Accédant : revenus 2015 + RFR
issue_body:
La simulation de test qui a tout déclenché : https://mes-aides.gouv.fr/tests/59b6548a8adbaa29787e41ed/show

Contexte : malgré les salaires entrés, l'allocation logement proposée est fantaisiste (dépasse le montant des mensualités de prêt, de plusieurs milliers d'euros).

Pistes de réflexion : 
- Dès qu'on commence à entrer les revenus 2015, le montant de l'allocation logement explose. En particulier, à partir du moment où on entre un revenu 2015 pour le conjoint qui n'a pas travaillé sur les douze derniers mois
- Le montant de l'allocation logement augmente avec les revenus 2015, comme s'il y avait un dépassement quelque part

Une trace manuelle pourrait peut-être trouver d'où vient l'erreur et corriger le calcul ? Cela a peut-être une influence sur d'autres calculs ?

Sylvain

issue_title: Duplicates in chomage variables ?

issue_title: Improve API of `RequestOptions` class
issue_body:
I find the new [`RequestOptions` class](https://github.com/algolia/algoliasearch-client-python/blob/master/algoliasearch/client.py#L48-L62) not very intuitive nor very safe.

IMHO, having a class is only useful if we are to do some typing/validation. But, in the current form, anything is accepted. Worse, there is no way to tell if some parameter is going to be a header or a URL parameter; instead, it's implicit: known parameters go as headers, others go as URL parameters. This has the following drawbacks:

- Adding an extra header is impossible
- Mistyping a header silently transforms it into a URL parameter without a warning

I would rather recommend the following approach (also used in other API clients):

- Accept two separate dictionaries in the constructor, for headers and URL parameters (basically mirroring the member fields)
- Provide named properties on top of them

issue_title: Support encrypting environment variables with KMS
issue_body:
I need to store sensitive data (e.g. passwords) for use by lambda. I'm currently planning on doing this by using a custom config file, but I see that Lambda supports KMS. Ideally I'm looking for a way that allows me to locally encrypt this data as well so I can safely store everything in my git repo (for example how ansible has a command to encrypt secrets using `ansible vault`) without adding extra tooling.

I plan on using separate accounts per stage to fully segregate my resources, so it'd useful if there was support for `config-<stage>.json` files as well. Doing that could allow these stage-specific config files to be encrypted separately per stage. It could require a password to decrypt the config for whichever stage while running/deploying.

Have you given any thought to how to handle encrypted config, and then push it up to lambda so it's encrypted there as well?

issue_title: Using LDAP auth breaks Google Authentication
issue_body:
Pre-conditions:
Google Auth is not the primary authentication system, base Centos 7 image is modified to use LDAP. 

Issue:
When a user attempts to login, due to the conditional in https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/95bf03a658cca834a129951c0ea702d813e38033/google_compute_engine/accounts/accounts_utils.py#L300-L305 Google Accounts daemon runs something akin to `getent` and normally `/etc/passwd` is checked. If the user does not exist in the local `/etc/passwd` then the user’s home directory is created and the ssh authorized_keys file is populated with the user’s public SSH keys from the GCP metadata. 

When using LDAP, the same `getent` will return that the user is already created on the box but the workflow does not check if the local home directory exists and whether or not the authorized_keys file is populated with the public SSH keys from the GCP metadata. This results in a scenario where the user is prompted to use their password for the current and all future ssh sessions. This is an inconvenience for end-users, breaks functionality for automation and provisioning tooling and raises security concerns.

issue_title: libsass miscompiles breadcrumb content
issue_body:
Under the environment:
```
sassc: 3.2.1
libsass: 3.2.4
sass2scss: 1.0.3
```
the SCSS in question:
```scss
content: "#{$breadcrumb-separator}\00a0";
```
is getting compiled to:
```css
content: "/\\00a0";
```
which is incorrect.
By comparison, Ruby Sass compiles this code to:
```css
content: "/ ";
```
which is correct (albeit not ASCII).

This is due to a bug in libsass: https://github.com/sass/libsass/issues/1115

The questions are (a) should bootstrap-sass try to workaround this? (b) if yes, how? (c) if yes, since this only affects libsass, do we make the change in bootstrap-sass, or in upstream Bootstrap?
CC: @twbs/sass

issue_title: Create a new ClasspathAssembleTask
issue_body:
Create a new `ClasspathAssemble` task which would depend upon IvyResolve's `exclusive_groups` AND PrepareResources `resources_by_target` AND create a Classpath data product.

Currently, IvyResolve has a hidden product.  It mutates exclusives_groups instead of consuming them and emitting a 'classpath' product for example. 

The only way to get resolve to mutate the exclusives_groups and be scheduled at all is to explicitly list 'resolve' on the command line or add a fake round_manager requirement for  **ivy_jar_products** or **jar_dependencies** -- we currently have these "fake requirements" added to any Task that needs IvyResolve to run before it (such as `JvmCompile`).

Once we create a new ClasspathAssemble task and have it emit a Classpath product, all the appropriate Tasks can then add `require()` on the new `classpath` product instead.
Something like,
    round_manager.require_data('classpath')

issue_body: We convert all 'unsafe' characters in the mock url paths to platform-safe characters that will work on windows or linux. issue_title: Make Sidebar pluggable
issue_body:
## Summary

Currently we hard code which URLs we expose in the main navigation sidebar.

Given that we have a plugin driven architecture, this is suboptimal, because only plugins that correspond to our anticipated plugin manifest will ever be exposed through the sidebar.

This also makes it difficult to implement the kind of suggestion made by @indirectlylit https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/1914#issuecomment-318434198 to implement a more material design style navigation system.

It is proposed that the sidebar be made pluggable for the following purposes:

- Bootstrapping SPA endpoints from the Django backend, to populate the main sidebar navigation items like 'Learn', 'Coach' etc. This would require the backend to supply properties such as: display name (internationalized on the backend), an identifier label. It would also require the design of some sort of ordering system, either based on 'priority' (i.e. how close to the top it should be), or 'preference' (where in the nav bar does it want to sit?). The latter system seems preferable to include the preference for appearing below the divider bar in the side nav. In either system, tie breaking could be delegated to alphabetical sorting on the label.

- SPAs on the frontend registering their sub navigation items to be displayed under their top level nav link on the sidebar.

issue_title: ipfs config may be located outside of ~/.ipfs
issue_body:
In `util.py` we read the `~/.ipfs/config` for both reading the IPFS configuration as well as writing the IPWB settings. Per `> ipfs --help`, the IPFS repository and thus configuration may reside elsewhere, as defined by the `IPFS_PATH` environment variable. By default, this variable is not set.

`readIPFSConfig()` and `writeIPFSConfig()` refer to `~/.ipfs/`without accounting for the potential for the IPFS repository to be elsewhere.

<strike>Strangely, if I `> ipfs init` after setting this variable (`> export IPFS_PATH=~/.ipfs2`), `~/.ipfs/` is created despite the variable's value.</strike> 

_UPDATE: Forgot `export` won't stick between shell sessions. Adding export to `.bashrc` and `ipfs init` produces content in `~/.ipfs2`. Original issue with ipwb still stands._

issue_title: AdWordsClient SOAP_IMPLEMENTATION_KEY ignored from YAML
issue_body:
In case I am using YAML file with authentication credentials and setting, I can't set the `SOAP_IMPLEMENTATION_KEY` (soap_impl) in the file.

`googleads.common.LoadFromString `and `LoadFromStorage` support this key and I can change the new default `'zeep'` to `'suds'`. But unfortunately the `googleads.adwords.AdWordsClient._OPTIONAL_INIT_VALUES` doesn't allow this option.

This option is very important for running AdWordsClient from serverless environment (specifically AWS Lambda). Zeep tries to create local SQLite files for cache in `$HOME` (even if I disable cache in client settings). But filesystem is read-only and the application crashes.

It seems to be enough just to add add 'soap_impl' to the list of optional attributes. In this case I can simply specify it in my config. Please advise if I should submit a pull request, or it's enough to report.
Thank you!

issue_title: Pipping using exec_create, exec_start stdin, stdout
issue_body:
If I use the docker CL this works perfectly for me.

    echo "hello" | docker exec -i $3 sh -c 'cat >/text.txt'

Now I want to use docker-py and have this so far:

    ex = cli.exec_create(container='nginx-ssl', cmd='cat >/carlskii.txt')
    print cli.exec_inspect(ex)
    ls = cli.exec_start(exec_id=ex["Id"], tty=True)

So how do I pass the "hello" or whatever data into the exec command so that it replicates the CL command ?

issue_title: potential for encodings in case names when running python3
issue_body:
I've noticed  an issued while testing the ark scraper in p2 and p3.

If you run it live (or against ark_example_2.)  via python2, then dump the json, you'll see a case with the name `Williams v. State ‎(2)`.  Do the same via python3 and the case name instead reads `Williams v. State \\xe2\\x80\\x8e(2)\\xe2\\x80\\x8e`.  

I'm wondering what the best fix might be.  I don't think it should be a fix on ark itself, but rather an upstream sanitation either in string_utils or maybe OpinionSite.

The case in question is the last one listed on this page (at the time of creating this issue): http://opinions.aoc.arkansas.gov/weblink8/Browse.aspx?startid=40626

issue_title: Race condition between routers and publisher
issue_body:
Changes in the apps, like deployment of new versions or scaling, are not instantly reflated to the routers which results in routers sending traffic to dead containers.

I manage to reproduce this using this simple scenario:
1. Build an app with the example-ruby-sinatra app
2. Make sure that there is 1 web process
2.5 Start a loop, requesting the app url 
3. Scale the down to zero using `deis scale web=0`
3.5 Stop request the app url

The logs collected between steps 2.5 and 3.5 are the following
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
```

The `200 OK` lines are expected, since the application is running. The `503 Service Temporarily Unavailable` are OK too since we scaled down to zero, thus there are no gateways. But the three `502 Bad Gateway` lines mean that nginx redirects traffic to servers that no longer exist.

issue_title: py-evm backend completely ignores fork setup
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

As of alpha 26, there is no clean way to define the VM configuration at runtime. `MainnetTesterChain.configure_forks` is broken: it sets the configuration on the vm instance, and then `get_vm_class_for_block_number` ignores `vm_configuration` on the instance, using the class's `vm_configuration` instead.

### How can it be fixed?

No one noticed that fork choice stuff isn't working. Maybe just neuter the API to *officially* make it do nothing, and we can re-add the functionality when it's requested.

Also, probably remove `MainnetTesterChain.configure_forks` from py-evm since it's described as "footgun" and isn't working anyway.

issue_title: BeeGFS hard-links
issue_body:
[BeeGFS](http://www.beegfs.com), a parallel cluster file system, [does not support](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/fhgfs-user/cTJcqGZceVA) `hard-links` between files in differents directories.

Depending on the [configuration](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/fhgfs-user/pvQSo0QWicw), either an error is issued, or a `symbolic-link` is created.

If a `symbolic-link` is created instead of a `hard-link`, it can cause problem, for example:

    pkgs/bin/mpicc
    pkgs/lib/libopen-pal.so
    envs/bin/mpicc
    envs/lib/libopen-pal.so

Here, when `envs/bin/mpicc` is executed, it is actually `pkgs/bin/mpicc` that is executed, and the library `$PREFIX/../lib/libopen-pal.so` actually loaded is `pkgs/lib/libopen-pal.so,` which is different from `envs/lib/libopen-pal.so` for which conda has fixed hard-coded prefix path, and as a final consequence `mpicc` fails to find its configuration file.

#805 is another (closed) related to `BeeGFS`.

Would we need a new conda options to turn hard-link into copy?

issue_title: ISY MAC address shows up in main ISY group (scene)
issue_body:
The latest versions if the ISY firmware, starting with 5.0.5 now add the ISY itself to the main ISY group:
```
<group flag="12" nodeDefId="InsteonDimmer">
  <address>XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX</address>
  <name>ISY</name>
  <family>6</family>
  <members>
    <link type="0">XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX</link>
    <link type="0">ADR0001</link>
...
```

This causes PyISY to die:

```
  File "../PyISY/PyISY/ISY.py", line 84, in __init__
    self.nodes = Nodes(self, xml=self.conn.getNodes())
  File "../PyISY/PyISY/Nodes/__init__.py", line 73, in __init__
    self.parse(xml)
  File "../PyISY/PyISY/Nodes/__init__.py", line 233, in parse
    Group(self, nid, nname, members, controllers), ntype)
  File "../PyISY/PyISY/Nodes/group.py", line 40, in __init__
    for m in self.members]
  File "../PyISY/PyISY/Nodes/__init__.py", line 314, in __getitem__
    raise KeyError('Unrecognized Key: [' + val + ']')
KeyError: u'Unrecognized Key: [XX:XX:XX:XX:XX:XX]'
Exception AttributeError: "'Group' object has no attribute '_membersHandlers'" in <bound method Group.__del__ of <PyISY.Nodes.group.Group object at 0xb5b50fec>> ignored
```

Since that device does not show up as a node anywhere else.   Should we just ignore group flag=12?  Or should we ignore a link in the group that is the same as the group address?
